Name: Lupton Route 66
Description: Historic eastern gateway where Route 66 enters Arizona from New Mexico, marking a key transition point for travelers entering the state.

Name: Painted Desert Inn Route 66
Description: Rustic Pueblo Revival inn built by the Civilian Conservation Corps in the 1930s, serving early Route 66 tourists exploring the Painted Desert.

Name: Petrified Forest Route 66
Description: One of the few preserved stretches of original Route 66 roadway located within a national park, reflecting early highway engineering and tourism.

Name: Wigwam Motel
Description: Iconic 1950s roadside motel featuring concrete teepee-shaped rooms, symbolizing mid-century automobile tourism and Route 66 novelty architecture.

Name: Holbrook Route 66
Description: Historic railroad and Route 66 town that developed as a service hub for travelers, known for its Wild West heritage and roadside motels.

Name: Jack Rabbit Trading Post
Description: Famous roadside attraction established in 1949, known for its “Here It Is” billboard campaign and classic Route 66 souvenir culture.

Name: Winslow Route 66
Description: Historic transportation hub where the Santa Fe Railway and Route 66 intersect, shaping the town’s role in regional commerce and travel.

Name: La Posada Hotel
Description: Restored 1930 Harvey House designed by Mary Colter, serving rail and Route 66 travelers with distinctive Southwestern architecture.

Name: Two Guns Route 66
Description: Abandoned tourist stop featuring ruins of trading posts, a zoo, and service stations that once catered to Route 66 motorists.

Name: Canyon Diablo Bridge Route 66
Description: Historic early concrete bridge carrying Route 66 across Canyon Diablo, representing early 20th-century highway engineering.

Name: Winona Route 66
Description: Small historic railroad stop and service community that supported Route 66 travelers between Flagstaff and Winslow.

Name: Flagstaff Route 66
Description: Well-preserved urban alignment of Route 66 through downtown Flagstaff, featuring historic storefronts, motels, and railroad influence.

Name: Museum Club Route 66
Description: Historic 1930s log building that served as a dance hall and nightclub, reflecting Route 66’s role in regional entertainment culture.

Name: Bellemont Route 66
Description: Historic railroad and Route 66 settlement that provided fuel, lodging, and services to travelers crossing northern Arizona.

Name: Ash Fork Route 66
Description: Stone quarry town known as the “Flagstone Capital of the World,” supplying materials used in Route 66-era construction.

Name: Williams Route 66
Description: Historic Route 66 town and gateway to the Grand Canyon, famously the last to be bypassed by Interstate 40 in 1984.

Name: Seligman Route 66
Description: Birthplace of the modern Route 66 preservation movement, where local efforts helped revive interest in the historic highway.

Name: Peach Springs Route 66
Description: Community on the Hualapai Reservation that preserves Route 66 heritage and serves as a gateway to the Grand Canyon’s western rim.

Name: Hackberry Route 66
Description: Historic general store and museum showcasing Route 66 artifacts, classic cars, and roadside Americana culture.

Name: Kingman Route 66
Description: Major transportation hub along Route 66, with strong ties to railroads, mining, and regional highway travel history.

Name: Cool Springs
Description: Restored gas station along Route 66 that once served motorists crossing the rugged Black Mountains.

Name: Sitgreaves Pass
Description: Winding mountain pass on the original Route 66 alignment, known for steep grades and dramatic desert landscapes.

Name: Oatman Route 66
Description: Historic mining town turned Route 66 tourist destination, famous for wild burros roaming its streets.

Name: Topock Route 66 Bridge
Description: Historic bridge marking the western Arizona crossing of Route 66 over the Colorado River into California.

Name: Testing poi
Description: Testing POI near current location for geofencing verification.
